summary: Teams should limit technology choices to three per category: a sensible default, an experimental trial, and a legacy option being retired. This constraint forces intentional decisions about adopting new technologies—before adding something new, teams must either complete retiring the legacy option or abandon the failed experiment. At organizational scale, this requires more coordination time but can be facilitated through tools like internal technology radars that communicate preferred technologies across verticals.
key_points: Limit each technology category to three options: default (current standard), trial (experiment), and retire (legacy being phased out)
Adding new technology requires first completing migration off legacy or abandoning failed experiments
This approach relates to the concept of 'innovation tokens'—limiting how much novelty a team takes on
Team-level decisions are easier due to trust and communication; org-level alignment takes longer
Publishing an internal technology radar helps communicate technology preferences across an organization
money_quotes: Within each normal-sized team, limit the choice of alternatives for any class of technology to three.
If we want to add another messaging tech then we have two choices. Either migrate all of our apps off the legacy tech, or properly rid ourselves of the failed experiment.
At a team level these kinds of limits are relatively easy to maintain and discuss and act upon, because we have common priorities and ways of working and high trust, high bandwidth communication.
key_insight: Constraining technology choices to default-trial-retire forces teams to complete their existing technology transitions before accumulating more complexity.
